coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots anchored onto mercaptopyridine-substituted zinc phthalocyanine-Au@Ag nanoparticle hybrid: Application as fluorescence “off-on-off” sensor for Hg2+ and biothiols
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Novel supramolecular hybrid containing polyethyleneimine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEI-GQDs) and mercaptopyridine-substituted zinc phthalocyanine (Pc)-Au@Ag nano-alloys is presented in this study. The designed hybrid was employed as a dual fluorescence nanoprobe for Hg2+ and biothiol detection in aqueous solution using PEI-GQDs as the fluorescence switching signal probe. The dual sensing platform for the analytes detection is firstly, based on the quenching (turn “OFF”) of the PEI-GQDs fluorescence upon π-π interaction or electrostatic attraction with Pc-Au@Ag conjugate. The quenched fluorescence can be switched back to the “ON” mode in the presence of Hg2+ and switched “OFF” again when biothiols are introduced to capture the Hg2+ ion via the formation of the strong metal-thiol bond (Hg-S). The “ off-on-off ” processes were modulated by different amounts of Hg2+ and biothiols. The nanoprobes were found to be highly stable and selective towards the target analytes in the presence of other amino acids and metal ions. Also, the probes were successfully deployed in the assay of the test analytes in spiked samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Au@Ag nano-alloys
Phthalocyanine
Fluorescence off-on-off
Mercury and biothiols

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of biothiols (Cys, Hcy or GSH)
Recovery studies (analytical applicability of the probes)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV/Vis absorption spectra of (A) Pc (a), Pc-Au@Ag (b), Au@Ag (c), and (B) PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag (a), PEI-GQDs-Pc (b) and PEI-GQDs (c). Solvent: PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0).
TEM micrographs of Au@Ag nanoparticles (A) and PEI-GQDs (B).
AFM image and height profile of PEI-GQDs (A), PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag (B), Au@Ag (C), and Pc-Au@Ag (D). Height profiles are for (A) and (B).
DLS graphs of Au@Ag (A), Pc-Au@Ag (B), PEI-GQDs (C) and PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag (D).
X-ray diffractograms of Au@Ag nano-alloy, Pc-Au@Ag conjugate and Pc alone.
EDX spectra of Au@Ag nano-alloy (A) Pc-Au@Ag conjugate, (B) and Pc alone (C).

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) PEI-GQDs alone and upon conjugation to Pc-Au@Ag conjugate. (B) PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag FL response (turn ON) signals in the presence of different amounts of Hg2+. (C) PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+ FL “switch OFF” signals at different concentrations of Cys. Solvent: PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0). λexc = 350 nm. Solvent: PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0).
UV-vis spectra showing changes in the ground state absorptions of Pc-Au@Ag alone (a) or in the presence of Hg2+ (b), Hg2+ and Cys (c, d). Solvent: PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0).

Selectivity study: (A) Bar charts showing the responses of PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag FL as probe for Hg2+ (25 nM) in the presence of different metal ions (100 μM) and blanks. (B) Fluorescence switching and quenching of PEI-GQDs in PEI-GQDs/Au@Ag-Hg2+ (probe 2) in the presence of Cys, Hcy and GSH (50 nM) premixed with some selected amino acids and proteins (500 μM). Solvent: PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0). a-k = lysine, alanine, cystine, cytochrome c, histidine, lactic acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, trypsin, tryptophan and glycine). Similar experimental conditions described in section 2.5 was employed for selectivity studies.
Synthetic route for the preparation of Pc-Au@Ag conjugate.
Illustrative description of fluorescence switching mechanism of PEI-GQDs upon non-covalent interaction with Pc-Au@Ag hybrid for Hg2+ and biothiols detection.
Optical and fluorescence parameters of PEI-GQDs and its Pc-Au@Ag hybrid. Mean size from DLS experiments also shown. PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in round brackets are the SPR band of Au@Ag. Values in square brackets are for PEI-GQDs. For the Pc, monomer maxima are listed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparative evaluation of the sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols by PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag probe with some reported methods in the literature.

row.entry: [4] for biothiols. This work for Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery studies of the target analytes (Hg2+ and Cys, as an example) in simulated samples as test of analytical applicability and reproducibility of the designed novel probes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots anchored onto mercaptopyridine-substituted zinc phthalocyanine-Au@Ag nanoparticle hybrid: Application as fluorescence “off-on-off” sensor for Hg2+ and biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel supramolecular hybrid containing polyethyleneimine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEI-GQDs) and mercaptopyridine-substituted zinc phthalocyanine (Pc)-Au@Ag nano-alloys is presented in this study. The designed hybrid was employed as a dual fluorescence nanoprobe for Hg2+ and biothiol detection in aqueous solution using PEI-GQDs as the fluorescence switching signal probe. The dual sensing platform for the analytes detection is firstly, based on the quenching (turn “OFF”) of the PEI-GQDs fluorescence upon π-π interaction or electrostatic attraction with Pc-Au@Ag conjugate. The quenched fluorescence can be switched back to the “ON” mode in the presence of Hg2+ and switched “OFF” again when biothiols are introduced to capture the Hg2+ ion via the formation of the strong metal-thiol bond (Hg-S). The “ off-on-off ” processes were modulated by different amounts of Hg2+ and biothiols. The nanoprobes were found to be highly stable and selective towards the target analytes in the presence of other amino acids and metal ions. Also, the probes were successfully deployed in the assay of the test analytes in spiked samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Supramolecular hybrids containing multi-functional materials are interesting due to the unique functionality introduced by the constituent materials and the fine tuning of the nanoparticle hybrid for desired applications. Nanomaterials have been surface or edge functionalized with various biomolecules, metallic nanoparticles, and macrocycles (such as phthalocyanines and porphyrins) to derive functional hybrids, tailored towards highly specific recognition of various target analytes [1–3]. We have reported on the detection of Hg2+ via “turn ON” using sulfur containing phthalocyanines (Pcs) in the presence of pristine graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Pc) [4]. A biothiol probe using Au@Ag nano-alloys in the presence of polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (represented as PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag) was also designed [5]. The grafting of PEI onto GQDs introduces positive charges on the surface of GQDs which is useful for electrostatic interactions; hence PEI-GQDs are employed instead of pristine GQDs. This work combines the two platforms to form PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag (represented as probe 1, the structure of the Pc is shown in Scheme 1 ), which allows for the detection of both Hg2+ and biothiols. The application of the fabricated supramolecular hybrid as fluorescence “ off-on-off ” probe for Hg2+ and biothiols with high sensitivity and selectivity is proposed for the first time. The fluorescence of the GQDs is quenched (“turned OFF”) within the PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag hybrid, but is restored (“turned ON”) in the presence of Hg2+, as PEI-GQDs are released to form PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+ (where the symbol/is used to show that PEI-GQDs are no longer linked). Then the fluorescence is “turned off” again by biothiols, hence the sensor is referred to as “ off-on-off ” system. Alloy bimetallic nanoparticles are employed in this work since they combine properties of individual metallic elements which are more interesting than single metallic nanoparticles as discussed previously [5].

It is expected that upon introduction of Hg2+, the Pc-Au@Ag complex could interact with Hg2+ to form Au+−Hg2+ metallophilic bonds, since it has been reported that Au+ ions are present on the surface of Au nanoparticles (NPs) [6]. Ag+−Hg2+ metallophilic bonds are also possible [6]. It is known that Au or Ag nanoparticles tend to aggregate in the presence of Hg2+ ion [7], hence aggregation of Pc-Au@Ag is expected following attachment of Hg2+. Due to the attachment of Hg2+, PEI-GQDs interaction with Pc-Au@Ag conjugate is perturbed (by disassembly), with a consequent detachment of PEI-GQDs from the Pc-Au@Ag surface (forming PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+). This results in the restoration of the quenched fluorescence emission, which is “turned OFF” again by biothiols. Hg2+ is known to bind more strongly to thiol compounds as compared to Au or Ag of the nano-alloys [8,9]. Hence, upon addition of biothiols to PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+ system (represented as probe 2, henceforth), the Hg2+ ions are expected to be desorbed and released from PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag surface, due to the formation of a stronger Hg-S bond. This leads to the possible disaggregation of the Pc-Au@Ag hybrid, and the reassembly of PEI-GQDs back onto the Pc-Au@Ag surface, with subsequent quenching of PEI-GQDs fluorescence. The extent of the quenched fluorescence was modulated by the concentrations of biothiols, hence affording the quantitative sensing of the biothiols. Thus, this platform can be used to detect Hg2+ and biothiols in a fluorescence signal “ off-on-off ” manner. The sensing ability of PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag for Hg2+ and biothiols is compared to that of individual components (PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag and PEI-GQDs-Pc, the latter for Hg2+ only).

The motivation to design nanoprobes for these analytes (biothiols) stems from the key roles they play when it comes to functioning of the cells and the physiology of the human systems [10]. Hence, methods for the rapid and sensitive detection of these biomolecules are desired. Functional hybrids for fluorescence detection of biothiols have been widely developed using metal nanoparticles (Au and Ag), G-quadruplex, bovine serum albumin (BSA)-Ag nanoclusters, graphene oxide-ruthenium-DNA composite, carbon nanodots and maleimide-functionalized GQDs [11–13]. But this work reports the first smart platform for the detection of both mercury and biothiols. Also, in the environment, Hg2+ is highly toxic and poses deleterious effects to human health and biota. Hence, the detection of this ion at nanomolar concentrations is highly imperative given their bioaccumulation tendencies in human and animal fatty tissues [14].

l-cysteine (Cys), dl-homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH), mercuric acetate, HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.9%, AgNO3, lysine, alanine, cystine and cytochrome c were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Histidine, lactic acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, trypsin, and tryptophan were obtained from Fluka. Glycine was purchased from SAARCHEM (South Africa). All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water obtained from a Mili-Q Water system (Millipore Corp. Bedford, MA, USA). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.0) was employed for all aqueous solutions. All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received. PEI-GQDs and Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles were synthesized as reported in literature [5] and as outlined briefly in supporting information. Electrostatic attraction between PEI-GQDs and tyrosine capped Au@Ag to form PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag has been reported before [5]. Quaternized 2,3-tetra-[(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato] zinc (II) was synthesized according to procedures previously reported [15].

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. Ground state electronic absorption was performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the range of 300–800 nm. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis was carried out using an INCA PENTA FET coupled to VAGA TESCAN using a 20 kV accelerating voltage. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 300, Picoquant GmbH) with a diode laser. Details have been previously provided [16]. A Metrohm Swiss 827 pH meter was used for pH measurements. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80 kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurement in tapping mode was carried out with MFP-3D Origin supplied by Asylum research (Oxford instruments company, USA). AFM analysis was done by drop casting and room temperature drying of the sample solutions on a freshly cleaved mica surface. X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, using Cu-Kα radiation (l = 1.5405 A˚, nickel filter). Data were collected in the range from 2θ = 10° to 100°, scanning at 1° min−1 with a filter time-constant of 2.5s per step and a slit width of 6.0 mm. Details have been previously provided [16].

The synthesis of PEI-GQDs-Pc conjugate was as outlined in literature [4], for this Pc but using pristine GQDs as follows: PEI-GQDs (20 mg, 4 mg/mL) were added to Pc (0.0002 g, 0.052 mmol) in dry DMF (5 mL). The mixture of PEI-GQDs and the Pc was ultrasonicated for 4 h, followed by stirring in the dark for 2 days. The green color of the Pc turned light green due to adsorption to the PEI-GQDs. Ethanol was used to repeatedly wash the product. The solid product was dried under vacuum overnight at 60 °C.

The Pc complex was conjugated to the Au@Ag nano-alloys via the interaction between the nano-alloys and the bridging sulfur (S) of the Pc. Electrostatic interaction is also possible using the quaternized N groups on the Pc (Scheme 1) and the negatively charged tyrosine capped Au@Ag. The tyrosine capping imparts negative charges onto Au@Ag.

Briefly: Au@Ag nano-alloy (0.002 g) and Pc (0.0002 g, 0.052 mmol) were dissolved in water (5 mL) and stirred for 96 h at room temperature for the adsorption of the Pc onto the surface of the Au@Ag nano-alloys. At the end of this time, the obtained product was centrifuged and washed with a mixture of ethanol and water in order to expel the non-conjugated Pc and Au@Ag nanoparticles, respectively. The obtained product was further air dried and subsequently characterized.

The non-covalent coordination (π-π stacking) of PEI-GQDs to the Pc in Pc-Au@Ag conjugate was achieved following procedures reported elsewhere [4] but with slight modification. Briefly: PEI-GQDs (0.005 g) were added to Pc-Au@Ag (0.0005 g in 10 mL of water). The resulting mixture was ultrasonicated for 4 h, followed by stirring in the dark for 48 h. Ethanol and hexane mixture (9:1) was used to repeatedly wash the product by slow centrifugation (5000 rpm for 5 min) followed by fast centrifugation (15000 rpm for 5 min) so as to remove the excess uncoordinated starting nanomaterials. The solid product collected was dried under vacuum overnight at 60 °C. The supramolecular hybrid is represented as PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag, probe 1.

Probe 1 (PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag, 0.3 mL (0.5 mg/mL)) and different amounts of Hg2+ (as mercuric acetate, final concentration: 1.0–100 nM) were mixed and diluted with aqueous PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.0) to a final volume of 3 mL, and incubated for 5 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at ambient temperature at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The spectral measurements were employed in the plotting of the calibration graph for Hg2+. Similar procedures were followed for the detection of Hg2+ using PEI-GQDs-Pc and PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag hybrid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of biothiols (Cys, Hcy or GSH)
ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+ solution (25.0 nM) was incubated with 0.3 mL of probe 1 (PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag) in PBS and this was followed by the fluorescence “switch ON” signal of PEI-GQDs in the presence of Hg2+ to obtained probe 2 (PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+). Afterwards, different concentrations of the biothiols were sequentially added to vials containing the probe 2 solution (final concentration: 1.0–200 nM, 3 mL). After an equilibration time of 5 min, the sequentially quenched fluorescence spectra were recorded at 350 nm excitation wavelength. The fluorescence quenching (F0/F) ratio versus the added concentrations of the biothiols was used to generate calibration plots for the biothiols. It should be noted that PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) was used throughout the sensing process and this is due to the fact that the fluorescence of PEI-GQDs is optimum at this pH, coupled with the desired physiological pH of 7.0 for the detection of the biomolecules. A complete list of sensors is shown in Table 1 .

To prepare Pc-Au@Ag conjugate, the nano-alloys were functionalized with the Pc by utilizing the strong binding affinity of the Au or Ag metal nanoparticles for the bridging S-atom of the Pc. Electrostatic interaction between the Au@Ag nano-alloys (with negatively charged surface due to the capping with tyrosine) and the quaternized N groups of the Pc (positively charged) is also possible [17]. Scheme 1 represents the conjugate formed.

The ground-state absorption spectrum of the Pc alone exhibited aggregation behaviour in water (Fig. 1 A(a)). Quaternized Pcs are known to be aggregated in water even though they are very soluble [15]. Aggregation of MPc complexes is evidenced by the presence of two non-vibrational bands at low and high energy, resulting from monomer and dimer respectively [18]. Aggregation increased for Pc-Au@Ag as judged by the increase in the intensity of the higher energy band (due to the aggregate). Upon conjugation of the Pc to Au@Ag nano-alloys, there were red-shifts in both the Q band of the Pc and the surface plasmonic resonance (SPR) band of the Au@Ag nano-alloys. The SPR band shifted from 473 nm for Au@Ag alone to 497 nm in Pc-Au@Ag, Fig. 1A and Table 1. The Q band for the Pc monomer shifted from 676 nm to 694 nm. Changes in the spectral features of Pcs in the presence of nanoparticles of Au or Ag are ascribed to the orientation of the molecular dipole of nanoparticles with respect to the Pc-nanoparticles complex as described previously [17]. Nanoparticles could exhibit red or blue shifts in their UV-Vis absorptions due to structural modifications in the presence of other compounds or molecules [7,17,19]. An observed shift in the SPR band of the Au@Ag within the conjugates from 473 to 497 nm could probably be due to some aggregation.

The loading of the Pc onto Au@Ag was investigated following a literature report [20] using absorption instead of fluorescence. This involves comparing the Q band absorbance intensity of the Pc in the conjugate with that of the initial Pc before conjugation. The loading of Pc onto Au@Ag was ∼11 μg Pc/mg Au@Ag NPs.

The TEM micrograph of Au@Ag nano-alloys shown in Fig. 2 A confirmed the spherical morphology of the nano-alloys with sizes averaging around 15 nm. The contrast features with core-shell like appearance show the successful formation of the nano-alloys of Au and Ag.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed to ascertain the size and shape of the Au@Ag nano-alloys before and after conjugation to the Pc (Fig. 3 ). There was an increase in the diameter of the Pc-Au@Ag (∼25 nm) compared to Au@Ag alone (∼13 nm), the latter is close to the value determined by TEM. The topographic image also shows that the Pc-Au@Ag nano-alloy's size became larger (Fig. 3D) and uneven in structure as compared to the round shaped and core-shell like architecture of the Au@Ag nano-alloys alone (Fig. 3C). This is an indication of the incorporation of the Pc onto the Au@Ag nano-alloys surface. Several Pc molecules could bind to the Au@Ag nano-alloys, larger surface based on size consideration. It is also possible that the Pc molecules on adjacent nanoparticles could associate by the known π-π interactions, resulting in aggregation [18].

The average particles size determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) was found to be ∼15 nm for Au@Ag (Fig. 4 A) which is in agreement with TEM and AFM results. In the case of Pc-Au@Ag, the average particle size was found to be 29 nm (Fig. 4 B), further showing an increase in the size of the Au@Ag nano-alloys due to the formation of Pc-Au@Ag conjugate. The DLS size for Pc-Au@Ag is not too far from that determined by AFM measurement at 25 nm. The powder X-ray diffractograms (XRD) of Au@Ag nanoparticles were quite similar to that obtained upon conjugation to the Pc, Fig. 5 . Both XRD patterns showed well-defined crystalline peaks attributed to 111, 200, 220, 311 and 222 planes which are in close agreement with the planes of face centered-cubic of metallic gold and silver [21]. However, the diffractogram of Pc-Au@Ag showed a broad peak occurring at 2θ = 14–32° due to the amorphous Pc [22] incorporated onto the Au@Ag surface. Elemental constituents of the Au@Ag nano-alloys and its Pc conjugate were deduced qualitatively via energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) as shown in Fig. 6 . Au@Ag alone before conjugation to Pc displayed peaks corresponding to Au and Ag, respectively (Fig. 6A). Additional peaks (such as C and Cl) resulting from precursor materials (HAuCl4 used for the synthesis of Au@Ag nano-alloy and tyrosine used for its capping [5]) could also be seen in the spectrum. Pc-Au@Ag conjugate displayed peaks which correspond to the elemental compositions of both Au@Ag nano-alloys and the Pc employed. This result further demonstrates the successful conjugation of the Pc to the Au@Ag nano-alloys (Fig. 6B).

The Pc-Au@Ag conjugate was then adsorbed onto PEI-GQDs to give PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag hybrid. The representation of the conjugate is depicted in the first part of Scheme 2 . The zeta potential data in Table 1 shows that Pc-Au@Ag is negatively charged whereas PEI-GQDs are positively charged, thus electrostatic bonding is possible between the two. In addition, adsorption is expected to occur through π-π stacking between the Pc and PEI-GQDs. There is also a possibility of the linking of NH2 group on PEI to Au@Ag. Thus, the hybrid has a combination of covalent and non-covalent bonds which will influence its sensing ability. Spectroscopic proof of the possible bonds is provided below.

In Fig. 3A and B, the AFM images of PEI-GQDs alone and when adsorbed onto the Pc-Au@Ag surface are shown. The height profiles are displayed under the AFM images (of A and B). We confirmed that Pc-Au@Ag hybrid interacted with PEI-GQDs using AFM by the observed change in the height profile of PEI-GQDs (1.7 nm, single to few layered GQDs) and 5.1 nm for PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag, indicating multi-layered components. This is considering the theoretical thickness of graphene layer of 0.34 nm [23].

This difference in height profile could be a reflection of the structural and topographic difference of PEI-GQDs upon coordination to Pc-Au@Ag conjugate. The topographic image (Fig. 3B) also shows that the supramolecular size of the PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag became larger as compared to the size of PEI-GQDs alone (Fig. 3A). This is an indication of the incorporation of PEI-GQDs onto the Pc-Au@Ag surface. DLS was employed for further characterization as shown in Fig. 4C and D. There was a significant increase in the size of PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag to 55 nm as compared to PEI-GQDs average size of ∼10 nm (Fig. 4C, a size of 10 nm was also obtained by TEM, Fig. 2B) or Pc-Au@Ag complex at ∼29 nm, Fig. 4B and Table 1.

The ground-state absorption spectrum of PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag hybrid (Fig. 1B(a)) showed increased aggregation (compared to the Pc alone) with the high energy (aggregate) peak being more intense than the low energy monomer peak, as was the case for Pc-Au@Ag. The increase in absorption below 550 nm is due to the absorption by PEI-GQDs and Au@Ag. The characteristic Q band (monomer peak) of the Pc component of Pc-Au@Ag was red-shifted from 694 to 697 nm for PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag, Fig. 1B, which is an indication of ground state interaction between PEI-GQDs and Pc-Au@Ag conjugate. There is also a red shift of the Pc Q band monomer peak in PEI-GQDs-Pc at 687 nm compared to Pc alone at 676 nm, Fig. 1B(b), with reduced aggregation (more intense lower energy peak). Flattening resulting in red shifts of porphyrins (similar in structure to phthalocyanines) upon conjugation to GQDs and graphene oxide have been previously observed [24]. The SPR band and PEI-GQDs absorption peak could not be properly observed for PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag. However, both the SPR band of the Au@Ag and PEI-GQDs absorption could be observed in PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag in the absence of the Pc, Fig. S1A.

At excitation wavelength of 350 nm, probe 1 (PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag) solution containing the supramolecular hybrid displayed almost complete quenching (turn “OFF”) of fluorescence of PEI-GQDs component when compared to PEI-GQDs alone (Fig. 7 A). The quenched emission was estimated to be about 95% in the PEI-GQDs hybrid. This very high quenching efficiency was presumably due to the dominance of the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged PEI-GQDs and negatively charged Pc-Au@Ag (as indicated by zeta potential in Table 1). The formation of N-metal (Au/Ag) bonds is also possible due to the presence of NH2 group of PEI-GQDs as well as π-π stacking between the Pc and PEI-GQDs. The same quenching behaviour was observed for PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag (Fig. S2A, supporting) and PEI-GQDs-Pc (figure not shown).

Fluorescence quantum yields (see supporting information for calculations) and lifetimes of PEI-GQDs upon coordination to the Pc-Au@Ag conjugate, Pc or Au@Ag were investigated in order to elucidate the kind of interaction and possible quenching dynamics of the emission of PEI-GQDs within the supramolecular hybrid. There were decreases in the fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) from 0.68 for PEI-GQDs to 0.25 for PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag, 0.14 for PEI-GQDs-Pc and 0.10 for PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag, Table 1, due to the quenching discussed above. This was accompanied by a corresponding shortening of lifetimes.

Excited state interactions of GQDs and Pcs and related molecules are known to result in non-radiative recombination of GQDs excitons with a consequent deactivation of the radiative recombination pathways, hence quenching of GQDs fluorescence [2,25]. As such, the observed fluorescence quenching of the PEI-GQDs could potentially mean that energy (or electron) transfer process had taken place upon coordination of PEI-GQDs to the Pc-Au@Ag or its components, as it is the case with other GQDs-macrocycles derivatives previously reported [2,16,25].

Fluorescence behaviour of the Pc upon conjugation with Au@Ag nano-alloys or Pc-Au@Ag conjugated to PEI-GQDs was not investigated due to the lack of photo-inactivity of the Pc and its derivative in water. Water-soluble quaternized Pcs are known not to be fluorescent in water due to aggregation which leads to the lowering of the photo-activity of the Pc molecules via dissipation of energy [15]. For this particular study, aqueous solubility is desirable for the detection of the test analytes (Hg2+ and biothiols). This is an added advantage of the designed probe since all the probe components are water soluble.

The fluorescence emission intensity of PEI-GQDs was “turned OFF” (Fig. 7A) upon conjugation to Pc-Au@Ag, Pc or Au@Ag as discussed earlier. However, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-GQDs within PEI-GQDs-Pc, PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag, and PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag hybrids was observed to gradually increase (“turn ON”) in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ in solution as depicted in Scheme 2 (Fig. 7B for PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag, and Fig. S2B for PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag), thus indicating that there are interactions between Hg2+ and Pc-Au@Ag, Pc or Au@Ag. The response plot of the “turn ON” detection of Hg2+ is shown in Fig. S3A constructed using the recovered fluorescence intensities in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of Hg2+ according to Eq. (1) [26].
(1)
Δ F
F 0
= 1 + K
[ H
g
2 +
]
where F and F0 represent fluorescence intensity in the presence or absence Hg2+, respectively, K is the fluorescence enhancement constant and [Hg2+] is the concentration of Hg2+. ΔF/F0, which denotes fluorescence enhancement is applied instead of F0/F normally used to indicate a fluorescence quenching process.

A linear dependency of ΔF/F0 towards Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.5–25.0 nM was observed (Fig. S3A, supporting information). The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was evaluated to be 0.25 for probe 1, Table 2 (see supporting Information on how LODs were evaluated). For PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag and PEI-GQDs-Pc, the LOD values were 2.45 and 0.82 nM, respectively, which are less favourable than that for PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag at 0.25 nM, showing the importance of combining the components in the latter. The LOD is indicative of high sensitivity of probe 1 towards Hg2+ detection at nanomolar range concentrations which is better or comparable to some reported methods, Table 1 [27–33]. We presume that the different forms of attachment of PEI-GQDs to Pc-Au@Ag which include electrostatic, NH2-metal or π-π stacking interactions that resulted in maximum FL quenching, also allowed for a high “turn ON” process induced by Hg2+ upon coordination to the Pc-Au@Ag surface. Hg2+ could coordinate to Au and Ag metals and the S containing Pc).

Further, it was quite interesting to observe that probe 1 (PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag), could be combined with Hg2+ (as probe 2, PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+) to detect biothiols. The same applies to PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag and the formation of PEI-GQDs/Au@Ag-Hg2+). This further brings another attractive application of PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag or PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag hybrids in the presence of Hg2+. The detection of biothiols is based on a mechanism of selective and competitive binding of Hg2+ between Pc-Au@Ag derivative and biothiols. Biothiols are mercury capturing molecules due to the very strong binding affinity of their S atom to the Hg2+ ions to form the robust Hg-S bonds with the biothiols [9]. Thus, biothiols such as Cys (used as an example, in Fig. 7C) can effectively abstract Hg2+ by the formation of the Hg-S bond, thereby disrupting the metaphilic attraction of the Hg2+ to the Au@Ag nano-alloys in PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag [9]. The same applies to PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag in the absence of the Pc.

As the concentration of Cys was gradually increased, the fluorescence of PEI-GQDs was “switched OFF” again by a steady decrease in the FL intensity of PEI-GQDs (Fig. 7C). Therefore, probe 2 (PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+) and PEI-GQDs/Au@Ag-Hg2+ could function as sensitive probes for Cys, Hcy or GSH detection via indirect “switch OFF” of PEI-GQDs fluorescence.

The linear calibration plot for the model biothiol Cys is shown in Fig. S3B. The LODs calculated for the biothiols are listed in Table 2. It can be observed from the obtained LODs that probe 2 is quite sensitive with nanomolar LODs for biothiols assay. These LODs are comparable to some other GQDs and non-GQDs based nanoprobes documented in the literature, Table 2. The LODs when PEI-GQDs/Au@Ag-Hg2+ (without the Pc) was employed were less favourable that for PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+) containing the Pc, Table 2, showing the importance of the presence of Pc.

The design of the probes was largely inspired from the knowledge of the performance of GQDs as excellent fluorophores and their fluorescence emission can be modulated by Pc, Au@Ag or Pc-Au@Ag within PEI-GQDs-Pc, PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag or PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag systems, respectively [16,34], in a “turn OFF” manner. Hg2+ gave a significant FL response (“turn ON”), Fig. 7B, which was attributed to the induced aggregation of the Au@Ag components of PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag, Pc-Au@Ag or PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag hybrids, as a result of the attachment of Hg2+ to the Au@Ag nano-alloys [9], There is also the possibility of Hg2+ coordination to the S-bridge of the Pc within PEI-GQDs-Pc or PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag systems.

The aggregation of PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag or PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag systems following addition of Hg2+ was proved by the addition of Hg2+ to Au@Ag nano-alloys alone. The recorded spectra as shown in Fig. S1B displayed a complete disappearance of the SPR band of Au@Ag nano-alloys as a result of aggregation and precipitation. Similar observation has been reported previously [6,35,36]. Also, upon the addition of Hg2+ to Pc-Au@Ag solution (displayed in Fig. 8 b), the spectrum was modified with a significant attenuation of the SPR absorbance of the Au@Ag component of Pc-Au@Ag complex. This could be as a result of the formation of aggregated particles [9,21], leading to changes in the surface states of the Pc-Au@Ag nano-alloys accordingly. The aggregation of the Pc increased following the addition of Hg2+ as judged by the increase in the higher energy peak compared to the Pc alone (Fig. 8b).

There were no spectral changes when Hg2+ was added to PEI-GQDs, Fig. S1C, showing no direct interaction between the two materials. Thus, the formation of the N-Hg2+ bond (N from PEI) may be ruled out (i.e. no bonds formed between N of PEI and Hg2+) during the “turn ON” process. The addition of Hg2+ to the Pc alone resulted in increased aggregation of the latter as judged by the increase in the lower energy band due to aggregation, Fig. S1D. Hg2+ is known to coordinate to sulfur which is present on the periphery of the employed Pc, resulting in the formation of the aggregates with decrease in the intensity of the Q band of the monomeric species followed by an increase in the aggregate peak, which has been similarly observed previously [37]. Thus, we can conclude in this work that Hg2+ coordinates with Au@Ag in PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag or PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag systems resulting in aggregation. Hg2+ also binds with the Pc component within PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag and PEI-GQDs-Pc systems to form S-Hg2+ bonds. Thus two binding modes (S-Hg2+ and metal-Hg2+) could be at play upon addition of Hg2+ to probe 1 (PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag) creating a higher chance of PEI-GQDs disassembly from the Pc-Au@Ag surface, resulting in probably more enhanced sensing.

Conversely, the addition of the biothiols (Cys, as an example) to the above mixture, in the presence of Hg2+ (PEI-GQDs/Pc-Au@Ag-Hg2+ or PEI-GQDs/Au@Ag-Hg2+) resulted in the shifting and attenuation of the SPR band of Au@Ag components of the probes, Fig. 8 (c and d).

In order to confirm that biothiols can also link to Au@Ag and not only Hg, biothiols (Cys shown as an example) were added to Au@Ag (Fig. S1E) or Pc-Au@Ag (Fig. S1F). This resulted in the attenuation of the SPR band of the Au@Ag nano-alloys, showing that the binding of biothiols to Au@Ag within PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag or PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag cannot be ruled out. Thus, when PEI-GQDs-Au@Ag or PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag were employed without Hg2+, biothiols could be detected but with lower sensitivity as compared to in the presence of Hg2+ [5], Table 2. Addition of biothiols to PEI-GQDs-Pc did not result in any spectral response, showing that biothiols coordinate to the metals Ag, Au or Hg metals within the conjugate systems. This spectroscopic evidence supports the fluorescence switching dynamics of PEI-GQDs in the presence of the target analytes.

To further explain the fluorescence switching of PEI-GQDs, exponential decay kinetics (fluorescence lifetimes) of PEI-GQDs were measured with the probe components (Fig. S4). Table S1 shows the recorded lifetimes of PEI-GQDs in PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag system after the addition of various amounts of Hg2+ and biothiols. As can be seen in Table S1, the lifetimes of PEI-GQDs increased close to its original value (up to 85%) upon the addition of Hg2+ due to the FL “ off-on ” process as explained above, but suffered a sequential decline upon the addition of the respective biothiols, which indicates that the FL “ on-off ” process is attributable to the re-assembly of PEI-GQDs onto Pc-Au@Ag surface and effectively blocking their radiative recombination pathways. These results further confirmed the influence of the analytes on the fluorescence “ on-off ” of PEI-GQDs when bound to Pc-Au@Ag conjugate.

To explore the selectivity of the probes for dual detection of Hg2+ and biothiols, we studied the effects of relevant metals ions that could competitively interfere in Hg2+ detection such as Fe2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Zn2+ and some amino acids in the case of biothiols. It was interesting to note that metals ions such as Cu2+ and Ag+ which usually interfere in the assay of Hg2+ by some reported methods [12,28] did not interfere in the assay for Hg2+ using probe 1 (Fig. 9 A) making it highly selective. Moreover, at tolerable error limits of 5%, experimental results showed that the co-existence of these metals as sample matrix may not influence the detection and quantification of Hg2+, which is a desired advantage of the designed probes. We conclude that the ability of Hg2+ to interact with the probe components (Pc and Au@Ag) as compared to other metal ions was responsible for the stronger response and thus Hg2+ is selectively preferred over other metals ions. On the other hand, some tested α-amino acids (glysine, lysine, histidine, tryptophan and dl-lactic acid) without thiol groups did not interfere in the quantitative assay of the biothiols. These results further contribute to the analytical figure of merit of the probes and show that probe 2 is specifically selective for the detection of thiols containing compounds which is ascribed to the stronger affinity of thiols to capture the Hg2+ ions bound to the Pc-Au@Ag complex surface and thus leading to the indirect quenching of PEI-GQDs fluorescence (Fig. 9B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery studies (analytical applicability of the probes)
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability and analytical precision of the fluorescence probes were evaluated following analytical recovery studies of spiked samples containing known concentrations of Hg2+ and biothiols, respectively. The analytical precision (batch to batch analysis) of a given assay is usually assessed by subjecting samples with known amounts of analytes through series of replicate analysis, followed by the precision calculations in terms of the relative standard deviation (RSD) (also known as percent coefficient of variation, % CV). The results shown in Table 3 show that the recovered Hg2+ was within the range of 98–106% (with RSD less than 5%) using the PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag system, probe 1. The percentage recovery (%) of Cys, Hcy or GSH (Cys used as an example) against known amounts in the spiked samples and the RSD evaluated from three replicate measurements, n = 3 (Table 3), further demonstrated the precision and reproducibility of probe 2 for biothiols detection in aqueous solution at physiological pH. These results further indicate that the PEI-GQDs-Pc-Au@Ag hybrid system could be devised as a potential probe for the analytical sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols commercially with good sensitivity comparable to other methods documented in the literature.

In conclusion, we have successfully synthesized novel supramolecular nanohybrid containing polyethyleneimine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEI-GQDs) and zinc mercaptopyridine-phthalocyanine Pc-Au@Ag nano-alloy conjugate. The supramolecular hybrid was further applied as simple and rapid fluorescence nanoprobes for the dual detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in an aqueous medium. The strategy was based on the fluorescence switching of the PEI-GQDs occasioned by “ off-on-off ” processes via the disassembly and re-assembly of the PEI-GQDs from the surface of the Pc-grafted Au@Ag nano-alloys, triggered by the target analytes due the stronger affinity between Hg2+ and biothiols than that between Hg2+ and the Pc-Au@Ag complex. The fluorescence switching of PEI-GQDs bestowed high sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor, which are comparatively better than some reported fluorescence probes. The practical applicability of the assays was further demonstrated by satisfactory recoveries of the target analytes in spiked samples. Hence, novel nanoprobes capable of nanomolar quantification of Hg2+ and biothiols in aqueous medium and at physiological pH have been developed in this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology and National Research Foundation, South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2017.06.002.
